articles.title: Rajasthan records 36°C for second day: Temperatures remain above normal; clear skies and sunshine forecast ... - Bhaskar English
articles.search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat") Assam
articles.full_text:
Rajasthan records 36°C for second day:Temperatures remain above normal; clear skies and sunshine forecast across state for next week
The weather remained clear in Sikar on Wednesday.
Rajasthan continued to experience rising heat as maximum temperatures crossed 36°C for the second consecutive day. With the sun’s intensity increasing, summer-like conditions have begun setting in across the state.
On Wednesday, most cities in western Rajasthan recorded maximum temperatures between 33°C and 37°C. Even nighttime temperatures remained elevated, with several cities reporting minimum temperatures of 16°C or above.
Weather experts have predicted clear skies across the state for the next week, accompanied by strong sunshine and a further rise in temperatures.
Western districts record above-normal heat
Over the past 24 hours, western districts experienced higher-than-normal temperatures. Cities including Jaisalmer, Jodhpur, Pali, Bikaner, Barmer, and Jalore recorded maximum temperatures above 33°C.
Barmer registered the highest maximum temperature in the state at 36.3°C — around 5°C above normal for this time of year.
The sun has started to get intense in Jaipur since morning. The feeling of heat has started during the day.
Morning and evening chill disappears
Following intense daytime heat, the mild chill usually felt during early mornings and evenings has largely vanished in several cities, including Jaipur and Bikaner.
On Wednesday, Jaipur recorded a minimum temperature of 18.2°C, Barmer 19.6°C, and Bikaner 20°C. Other minimum temperatures included 17.8°C in Dungarpur, 17.1°C in Pratapgarh, 16.6°C in Chittorgarh and Udaipur, 16.3°C in Jaisalmer, and 16°C in Jodhpur.
With clear weather expected to persist, residents are likely to experience steadily rising temperatures across Rajasthan in the coming days.

articles.title: Bengaluru Weather Warning: 39°C Heat May Hit Soon As IMD Predicts Super Hot Summer & Heatwave From March - Goodreturns
articles.search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat") Assam
articles.full_text:
Bengaluru Weather Warning: 39°C Heat May Hit Soon As IMD Predicts Super Hot Summer & Heatwave From March
Bengaluru weather update: The city is on the brink of a scorching summer, with the India Meteorological Department predicting above-normal temperatures and possible heatwave conditions from March 2026. While no active heatwave alert has been issued for Karnataka weather today, February 26, IMD has warned that the coming months could bring harsher-than-usual conditions across Bengaluru and several other districts.
According to IMD projections, summer in Bengaluru will officially begin on March 1, with temperatures expected to jump during April and May.
Bengaluru Weather Today, February 26th
As of this morning, Bengaluru's temperature today stands at around 21°C, with humidity levels at 83% in the early hours. The city is covered with clear skies, and the day is expected to remain partly sunny, with the maximum temperature expected to be around 32°C.
As per data from Ease Weather, Bangalore weather in February began with average highs of 30.1°C, gradually rising to nearly 31.7°C toward the end. So far, February 2026 has been slightly cooler than historical averages by about -1.1°C, but temperatures in the coming days are forecast to spike to 34.1°C toward warmer-than-usual conditions.
Historically, Bengaluru weather has recorded extreme summer peaks. The city touched 39.2°C in April 2016, while May's all-time record stands at 38.9°C (1931). IMD data suggests that the city could once again approach the 39°C mark in the coming months.
Heatwave Risk In Karnataka From March To May
While no heatwave alert in Karnataka has been announced as of now, IMD forecasts say that there is a high probability of above-normal maximum temperatures statewide. Coastal regions have a 65-75% probability of temperature spikes, while northern interior Karnataka may face the highest risk of severe heatwaves during peak summer.
Districts such as Bagalkot, Koppal, and Belagavi in north interior Karnataka are likely to experience intense heat. South interior districts, including Mysuru and Davangere, are also bracing for elevated temperatures. Coastal areas like Dakshina Kannada, Udupi, and Uttara Kannada already have temperatures touching 37-39°C due to high humidity levels.
For Bengaluru weather in March 2026, IMD expects daytime heat and hotter-than-usual nights.
Heatwave Precautions for Residents
Taking the Bengaluru heatwave prediction for 2026 seriously, authorities have issued an advisory for residents to prepare for the tough summer ahead.
- Monitor official updates via the IMD official website and social media channels.

articles.title: Scientists strengthen pure metals using extreme heat - Digital Journal
articles.search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat") Assam
articles.full_text:
`Oumuamua seems to be a dark red highly-elongated metallic or rocky object, about 400 metres long, and is unlike anything normally found in the Solar System.
Source - ESO/M. Kornmesser, CC SA 4.0.
Scientists have developed a new means to strengthen metal in extreme conditions. Whilst it has been long understood that heating a metal softens it, counterintuitively, heat strengthens pure metal in extreme conditions. This could lead to better designed metals for applications in extreme conditions, including outer space.
The findings come from Northwestern University engineers, and this challenges long-held assumptions of how metals behave. The researchers found that if you heat a pure metal and attempt to deform it at extremely high speeds, it flips. The opposite happens and the metal strengthens, resisting the deformation.
Pummelling metals with tiny particles
At everyday speeds, metals deform — meaning they bend, stretch or dent — in ways that scientists understand well. Heat helps atoms move, making metals softer and easier to shape. But when deformation happens extremely fast — in millionths or billionths of a second — those same rules no longer apply.
Since conventional tests cannot reach these extreme conditions the scientists turned to an unconventional approach. They used a specialized technique that blasts hard, microscopic particles at speeds up to hundreds of meters per second. At these speeds, the tiny particles ballistically impact the metal, stretching the metal to 100 million percent of its original length in one second.
The researchers also performed the experiment with metal samples ranging from high purity to slightly alloyed versions of nickel, titanium, gold and copper and from temperatures ranging from room temperature up to 155 degrees Celsius.
The results revealed a stark divide. As temperatures increased, pure metals became stronger and harder. Alloyed metals, however, behaved typically — becoming softer when heated.
For decades, engineers have added impurities (or alloying elements) to metals to make them stronger. Pure iron, for example, is soft and bends easily. But adding carbon transforms iron into steel — a metal strong enough to support the world’s tallest skyscrapers and bridges that can hold millions of tons of weight across their lifetimes.
It would appear atomic vibrations are responsible for this counterintuitive behaviour. If a particle slams into a pure metal at an extreme speed, it meets resistance from the metal’s vibrating atoms. At any given moment, some atoms are vibrating in a direction that opposes the deformation. As the temperature increases, those vibrations intensify, making it harder for the fast-moving particle to deform the metal’s surface. So, the metal becomes stronger.
In alloys, impurities act as roadblocks that also resist deformations. In that case, heating the metal gives defects the energy to overcome these obstacles, restoring the typical hotter-is-softer behaviour. Adding just 0.3% alloying elements was enough to completely reverse the metal’s counterintuitive response.
Purity as a materials design parameter
These findings have implications for technologies that operate under intense heat and extreme strain rates. By heating a pure metal, it could become more resistant to sandblasting, ballistics and hypersonic speeds. Engineers also could tune a metal’s response to high temperatures by adjusting its purity.
The research appears in the journal Physical Review Letters, titled “At extreme rates, pure metals thermally harden while alloys thermally soften”.

articles.title: Carnarvon banana supply hit for 18 months after heatwave and cyclone - FreshPlaza

Carnarvon banana supply hit for 18 months after heatwave and cyclone
Banana growers in Carnarvon, Western Australia, are assessing damage following a January heatwave and a February cyclone, with some estimating it may take 12 to 18 months for stock levels to return to normal.
The region, often referred to as Western Australia's food bowl, experienced three consecutive days above 40 °C at the end of January, with a peak of 47.9 °C recorded on January 21. The heat burned crops and caused fruit drop. On February 10, a system downgraded from Tropical Cyclone Mitchell crossed the coast, bringing wind gusts of up to 107km per hour.
Banana plantations were among the hardest hit. Growers estimate losses ranging between 20 per cent and 60 per cent across farms, with some reporting crop losses of 40 per cent to 50 per cent following the cyclone.
Banana grower Doriana Mangili said the larger plants, typically harvested at this time of year, were most affected. "We're seeing very low volumes at the moment, like we're packing in a week what we could normally pack in a day at this time of year," she said.
"The next couple of months are going to be really tough for our growers and for our staff, and really for the next 12 to 18 months," she said.
According to Mangili, extreme heat events have become more frequent. "When I first came to Carnarvon, (heatwaves) were pretty rare. We had one, I think in 2007, and then we didn't have another one for like, five to 10 years. We seem to have had an extreme heatwave every year just about, for the last three or four years," she said.
Growers are focusing on replanting in March and diversifying production, including freeze-dried bananas, to maintain operations during the recovery phase.
Supply volumes to supermarkets are expected to remain below typical seasonal levels as crop regeneration and new plantings progress.

articles.title: Floods, Heatwave & Upwelling Linked to Algal Bloom - 5MU
articles.search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat") Assam
articles.full_text:
Nearly a year after a harmful algal bloom first appeared off South Australia’s South Coast, authorities say preliminary research points to a “convergence” of environmental factors behind the unprecedented outbreak.
The bloom, driven by the dinoflagellate Karenia, spread across coastal waters for months and remains active in parts of the state.
At a meeting of the state’s Algal Bloom Taskforce this week, marine scientist and South Australian Research and Development Institute executive director Mike Steer said three anomalous events had “primed the environment” for the bloom: River Murray flooding, a significant cold-water upwelling event and a marine heatwave.
Article continues after this ad
Advertisement
Professor Steer said the combination of nutrient loading and calm, stratified ocean conditions created an ideal environment for the organism to thrive.
Environment Minister Lucy Hood said the preliminary findings were consistent with what government scientists had previously indicated, describing the bloom as the result of multiple overlapping pressures rather than a single trigger. She said the research would now undergo peer review before being publicly released.
Estuarine ecologist Faith Coleman, who resigned from two state advisory panels earlier this year, has questioned the suggestion that River Murray flooding three years ago significantly contributed to the event.
Dr Coleman said she had not seen evidence linking those flood-driven nutrient loads to where the bloom first developed, arguing other sources such as upwelling and stormwater may have played a larger role. She has called for the report to be made available once peer review is complete.
Authorities say the only active bloom area is currently along the south-west Yorke Peninsula, between Point Turton and Port Victoria, with monitoring showing low or no levels at metropolitan sites.
Article continues after this ad

articles.title: Why do deserts have so much sand if it hardly ever rains? Wind, erosion, extreme heat, and millions of years transforming rocks into giant dunes that never stop moving - CPG Click Petróleo e Gás
articles.search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat") Assam
articles.full_text:
Why do deserts have so much sand if it hardly ever rains? Wind, erosion, extreme heat, and millions of years transforming rocks into giant dunes that never stop moving.
Wind, erosion, dunes, and millions of years help explain why the desert accumulates so much sand even with very little rain.
The classic image of the desert with seemingly endless dunes hides a far more complex process than it appears. Over millions of years, Rocks were broken, crushed, and carried away by the wind. ...until they are transformed into fine grains, which today form some of the most extreme landscapes on the planet.
When someone asks why there is so much sand in the desert if it hardly ever rains, the answer lies not in the lack of rain, but in the combination of factors. Dry climate, intense erosion, persistent winds, and enormous geological timescales.What today appears to be a sea of sand began as solid rock, exposed to the sun, cold, and winds for ages.
Desert is not synonymous with sand.
Despite the immediate association, Most of the world's deserts are not made up of dunes.Instead, it consists of exposed rocks, pebbles, gravel, and hardened soil. In many places, the desert is a vast field of stones, not a carpet of soft sand like in the movies.
Sand dune areas occupy only a fraction of these environments. They emerge where the desert offers the right combination: sediment sources, strong winds, and little or no vegetation. to hold the soil in place. In these special zones, the sand is able to accumulate, reorganize itself, and form large, mobile structures.
The desert sand didn't appear all at once. It is born from Slow and continuous erosion of rocks, in a process that takes thousands and even millions of years. In desert regions, extreme temperature variations work against the rocks all the time.
During the day, the heat causes the rock to expand. At night, the sudden drop in temperature causes it to contract. This daily cycle ultimately generates... cracks, chips and fragments, which detach themselves from the larger structures.
Over time, these pieces of rock break down more and more, until they approach the typical size of grains of sand.
The wind takes center stage in this phase. As it blows, it It lifts, drags, and makes the grains collide with each other., rounding off the particles and further wearing down the material.
Each impact removes small portions of rock, thinning and shaping the sediment until it is light enough to be transported over long distances.
How the wind shapes the desert over millions of years.
The wind doesn't create the sand, but selects and organizes what remains after erosion.Very large grains cannot be carried easily, so they remain closer to their origin.
Very fine particles, such as dust, can be lifted and transported for hundreds of kilometers, leaving the original area almost clean.
What remains in the desert is a "middle ground": grains of ideal size to be dragged along, bounce close to the ground, and be deposited a little further on.
This repeated movement doesn't happen over days or months, but rather for immense periods of geological time, in which small amounts of sediment accumulate and are reorganized.
Throughout these endless cycles, Entire fields of dunes emerge, recede, advance, and change shape., depending on the direction and intensity of the winds.
Visually, the desert appears static, but on a scale of decades and centuries its forms are constantly changing.
Why does so much sand accumulate in certain parts of the desert?
Not every desert turns into a sea of dunes. For that to happen, several factors need to work together for a long time:
In regions with a large availability of exposed rocks, erosion breaks the solid material into increasingly smaller fragments.
Next, the wind takes care of... The sand will concentrate where there is not enough vegetation to hold the soil., pushing the grains towards areas of more favorable terrain.
Over time, These deposition zones turn into veritable reservoirs of sand., where the dunes grow, advance and reorganize themselves without stopping.
Even without rain, the constant flow of wind ensures that the landscape remains vibrant, with shapes that shift slowly but never stay exactly the same for very long.
Little-known facts about the desert
When people talk about deserts, many imagine only heat and sand, but some details help to better understand this type of environment.
In so many cases, Desert sand tends to be more rounded than beach sand.precisely because it spends so much time in motion, pounding and scraping grain against grain.
Another curiosity is that Not all sand is the same.Composition and color vary according to the type of rock that gave rise to the grains. There are deserts with lighter, darker, and even reddish sand, resulting from specific minerals present in the original material.
In some places, The dunes once occupied areas that were once the seabed or even forests.leaving behind traces of environments very different from the present one.
We also pack any The desert dunes are always in motion....even if this displacement is imperceptible in a single day. Over the years, they can completely change position, swallow roads, advance on cities, or recede, leaving behind surfaces previously covered by sediment.
What science explains about deserts.
Science seeks to understand the desert precisely because it is an extreme environment and at the same time very sensitive to climate change. Processes such as erosion, weathering, sediment transport, and wind dynamics. They help explain why these places accumulate so much sand.
In educational content, such as that produced by science communication channels, desert formation is presented as a result of... Millions of years of combined action between dry climate, scarce water, constant wind, and slow transformation of the Earth's crust..
The difference between sandy and rocky deserts is often used to show that the landscape we see today is just a snapshot of a much longer history.
The desert as a living record of the planet's history.
The large amount of sand in the desert is not a sudden phenomenon, nor a "mystery" linked to the lack of rain.
It is the end product of an almost infinite sequence of natural processes., which begin with the breaking of rocks and end with the organization of grains into giant dunes.
The desert, which at first glance appears empty, functions as a natural archive of Earth's geological historyEach dune, each layer of sand, and each field of stones records a fragment of the interaction between climate, relief, wind, and deep time.
Far from being static landscapes, deserts are constantly transforming environments where the sand never stops moving.
And you, when you look at a desert, what impresses you the most? The vast expanse of sand, the silence of the place, or the idea that completely different environments once existed there?

articles.title: 'Ridiculous': Lake users criticise $208k public loo upgrade - Otago Daily Times
articles.search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat") Assam
articles.full_text:
A planned $208,000 revamp of the public toilet block at Canterbury's Lake Heron will see the three flush loos and urinal upgraded to two unisex dry vault toilets.
But long-time users of the area, Brenda and Murray Beach, said the Ashburton District Council's planned replacements are not enough for the popular site.
Brenda said over the summer holidays at least 31 caravans were on site, plus a steady stream of casual campers, tourist day trippers, the occasional minivan and bus.
"The facilities are over worked during the summer, and to us it seems ridiculous to take that away and supply only two toilets," she said.
The Ashburton couple would like to see the toilets "upgraded to more and better facilities".
District council group manager community and open spaces, Toni Durham, said the current septic tank system and long drop are causing problems for the surrounding waterways and Lake Heron.
"Dry-vault toilets avoid these issues because they don’t produce wastewater," she said.
"They’re a more environmentally friendly option, and we’ve seen them work well in other high-country and foothill locations like Bowyers Stream, Taylors Stream, Awa Awa Rata Reserve, and Lake Camp."
The plan includes the removal of the old toilet block, which had separate men’s and women’s toilets and a long drop.
"These will be replaced with a brand-new block that has two unisex toilets," Durham said.
"They’ll be open all year round. The new toilets are dryvault systems, which don’t need a sewer connection and are much lower maintenance."
She said the council agreed to fund the project in the 2024-34 Long Term Plan.
"The cost is $208,000, which covers removing the old toilets and three septic tanks as well as installing the new facilities."
Durham said, although there were fewer actual toilet units on this site, the overall toilet capacity for the Lake Heron area was increasing. The new toilets would be open all year round, not just seasonally, and unisex toilets allow more flexibility and efficiency compared with gendered ones.
"The Department of Conservation has also added new toilets at the southern end of the lake since council last built facilities there."
Durham said the new toilet block has already been built off-site and is just waiting to be delivered.
Work on the installation was expected to be completed by the end of May.
